sentences.text: Yo, what's up, Justin?
Not too much, how you doing?
Yeah, man.
Trying.
Trying to close some deals like you, my friend.
I wish.
Get some small ones.
Yeah.
I feel like that's, like, all mine have been as well, like, small little triples, but… Yeah.
That's kind of all we can do.
Yeah, it's… I got, like, a 4K a month quota, so it's… Never.
Yeah, it's, like, never done in, like, one swoop.
It's always gonna be, like, four deals, basically.
It sucks.
Yeah.
I think you, you closed one yesterday, right?
Yeah, it was one that I was working for a bit.
Yeah, $1500, so it was good.
Like, 7 users or something.
Nice.
Yeah.
Yeah, we'll take that.
Were you on the, … Scott joined, I'll let him in in, like, 30 sec- or, like, 15 seconds.
Were you on the, … you were on the Monday call yesterday, right?
Yeah.
God, I felt bad for Shane when he was like….
the CBRE thing, I went and looked, there's, like, 20 closed ops, and then he won one that was, like, 500.
Yeah.
I had to tell him, I was like, I can't get on these demos anymore, man.
We were doing, for real, like, 10 a week for, like, a month, and I was like, I can't do them anymore.
I'm like, you're on your own.
I'm like, it's just… kills my time, and I was like, I felt bad for him, because it was so much work, man.
I had to bow out, though.
I'm like, I'm not doing it anymore.
Especially if it's, like, going nowhere.
Right?
Yeah, damn.
Okay, so we've met with Scott a handful of times.

They have a super old owner who refuses to change anything, but I think he is now either out of the picture or retiring, so he had reached back out to… they do everything manual, so kind of just see what's been going on there.
Awesome.
Morning, Scott, how are you?
I'm good, how are you?
I am doing good.
Good, and I've got Scott DeMasio on here as well.
Hey, guys.
Oh.
It's, nice to reconnect with you.
Yeah, absolutely.
It's been a little while, huh?
Yeah, it's been, … I don't know, maybe… maybe we met earlier this year, but I know last year for sure we had met.
What's, what's been going on professionally and personally with, the both of you?
Will?
Let's see, I mean… I would say generally same old, same old for our operation here.

I mean, we're dealing with the market, you know, that everyone's dealing with in the Sun Belt, meaning, you know, plenty of supply, it's pushed down our Revenue's a bit, NOI's down a bit, but, you know, the way that we're structured, we're able to handle that fine.

We're… lowly leveraged, all of our assets are long-term holds and stuff like that, so, kind of pushing along and just learning how to deal with that and making our pricing adjustments, you know, implementing some concessions and things like that that we haven't done historically.

… But, and, you know, trying to take on some new projects, here and there, While we, you know, we're not aggressively going after any acquisitions right now, so we're, you know, kind of internally trying to make some… Minor, but relevant changes.
Hence, you know, us reaching out to you guys and just seeing how things are going.

We haven't made any absolute decisions moving forward with, gaining additional market data, but that's kind of where we are right now, just revisiting some of these things that we've looked at in the past.
Yeah, for sure.
I think that makes sense.

And, I think, … I think the past couple times we've met, I think there's just been some pushback from, I think, just, like, the owner of trying to, kind of, automate some of these, you know, market… market surveys, just overall market data collection.
Is that still the case?
Yes.
Okay.
Well, that's, that's still a hurdle that, I can't wait to get over at some point, but, yeah, I think today's call will be productive.

I think we have a handful of new updates and, some different reporting that we have rolled out, since you last took a look, so, … We could do, … we could do an entire kind of overview and refresh for you both, if that makes sense, and then also kind of touch on some of the new updates that we've done.
Does that work for you?
Yep, of course.
Okay, awesome.
Cool.
Yeah, I'll take you through it.
And I guess… Scott, nice to meet you for the first time.
I guess I'm… are you familiar with the tool, or do you understand how it kind of works?
I don't know if, Scott Ferguson, if you filled him in, or I guess, where would it make sense to start?
Have you… you haven't seen this before.
I haven't seen it.
I mean, I've seen… Very little in line.
I mean, you can do your, you know, your 10,000-foot dive and then jump in.
Yeah, for sure.
So, I think, just so you're aware, I mean, what you're about to see, everything's fully automated, the data's updated every day, and we have near-perfect coverage.
I mean, we can track anybody with an online presence.
But you'll see here in a second, it's primarily rent comp data, but we also have things like construction pipeline, demographic data.

We present it a few different ways, so the first way here, you know, operationally, the My Properties section, this would be, like, your market surveys, so comparing your property versus its direct competitors.
But we do have market, sub-market data, we have, ownership information, transactional data, cap rates, we can give you, like, rent rules, you know, forecasting coming very soon.
So, I think what's exciting with the tool here is that, you know, this is kind of becoming that one-stop shop for everything multifamily.

Very biased, but I think that we have the best rent comp data already, so now it's completing the picture with some of those newer features that I was mentioning, and really just making ApartmentIQ kind of the primary source from everything from operations to acquisition, development, and then just understanding even your market even better, so… That's kind of the high level, but … I'd say final thing just to be aware of is that everything inside of ApartmentIQ is 100% publicly available data.
So, our primary source of truth for rent comp data is going to be property websites, but more specifically, the online leasing section of a property website.
So, we're tracking about 40 million….
You guys just have, like, agent crawlers that are just scouring data daily, basically?
Yep, exactly.
Yeah, so we use, crawlers, and then we also use, like, a large language model to go out there and track rent specials and concessions.
So, we use the AI to basically parse the concessions out, apply them to appropriate floor plans and units, and then calculate that effective rent for you.
But, yeah, exactly right.

And maybe you're gonna get into this, I don't want to jump ahead too far, but… since, you know, we've just kind of… historically, we haven't done a lot of concessions, and recently we've implemented, quite a few, just because we've recognized we need to be… when you guys are, you know, presenting this data on, you know, for my properties and the comps and everything, is it going to show you kind of, like, both gross and then net or net asking after… after the concessions?
Yep, exactly.
Okay, cool.
Yeah, and we can take, … Gross and net rents all the way down to the actual unit level.
We also have 5 years' worth of historical data, so you can look at trends and seasonalities of both the gross and net rents.
You can also look at concession history.

So, what's interesting, I know, you know, concessions are a bit of a newer game in town for you guys, so what some of our customers will do is they'll actually take a look at concession history, and they'll look at, concession history and overlay it with leasing velocity.
And they'll basically say, like, hey, if a property offers 1 month free, for example, another property offers 2 months free, what's that delta?
You know, how many leases did that extra concession, or that extra month concession, you know, yield them?
Yep.

There's some, too, that, you know, if… you kind of, you know, there's groups out there that want to be the first ones to offer concessions in the market, so you can actually look at concession history.
As well as, like, occupancy percentages, and see, like, what level of occupancy triggers a property to offer a concession.
Is it whenever they hit 90%, or is it whenever they're at, you know, whatever percentage?
So you can get a sense of, kind of, how the market's operating, and be able to kind of gauge on, you know, whether or not these concessions are going to be coming.
Cool.
Yeah.
But yeah, I'll take you through it.
So, three sections.
The first one here, again, this is the market surveys.
The way this is laid out is that each one of your properties would get their own individual property card, and then all of the data is based off of that property-specific comp set.
Just so you're aware, whenever we set these things up, typically our customers will say, you know, hey, here's who we've been tracking, and we'll start there.

However, we do have a comp matching algorithm, so we can make some suggestions or recommendations on who we think you might want to track or follow, but what's great is that in the application, you guys can always add or remove a property to your comp set anytime you'd like.
So, complete control of the comps.
I'll also mention that we do offer unlimited users, so we can give access to whoever's relevant to a property, site teams, regionals, marketing, operations, revenue, etc.
And there are different permission levels, so if you only want to give access to a site team, we can make it that they see their property, or a regionals sees their portfolio, etc.
But if you click into a property, once you're in here, the very top, this will be your main navigation.
So, each property does have four reports associated with it.
Again, I'll give you the high level of each one.
Before I do.
All the data's updated as of this morning, but what's nice is with this dropdown, we do let you aggregate it over whatever time frame is relevant to your conversation.
We also have PDF and Excel reports that can be downloaded whenever you'd like.
They can also be automated to be delivered to an inbox on whatever frequency or cadence you'd like as well.
Okay.
We also have quite a few filtering options, so you can manipulate the data pretty much however you'd like.
Bedroom-bathroom combos, square footage ranges, rent price ranges.
If you'd like, you can even filter in or out renovated units, classic units, penthouses, affordable units.
Down below here on the left, all of these metrics are sortable from low to high and high to low.
The market survey, which we're on, is broken out into five sections.
The first one's property summary, so this is your high-level property data.
So this is where you can see, you know, vintage, who manages the property.
We give you links to the Google reviews.
Down below here, you can see, again, if there is a concession, if it's tied to the property, or even specific floor plans or units in the property.
We can present concessions two different ways.
So one is a percentage of rent, and the other two is… the other way is just based off of value.
And then above it, as you can see, we'll give you both gross and net rents at the property level.
Okay.
If you keep scrolling, the second section is going to be those performance metrics.
So your occupancy, lease exposure percentages.
We hit on availability, and then we even show things like applications, cancels, denials, and leases.

For my demo account, I'm showing you these metrics over the last 7, you know, with a 7-day horizon, but know that we have customizable metrics, that you can look at them over the last 30 days, or the last, you know, 2 months, or the last 3 months.
So there's a lot of different ways that you can present the data.

And what I like about this, and again, just all this data being publicly available information, is if your team ever has a question about where any data point metric calculation's coming from, we can trace it all back to the source.
You can also… sorry, go ahead.
Sorry to cut you off.
On, like, for the occupancy there, and then the lease, and the exposure, all that good stuff, where do you guys pull that if this is publicly available?

Yeah, so it all has to do with the fact that we're tracking unit-level data every single day, so we can just do some math based off of the units that we find, and then, you know, calculate it based off the total units in the property.
Okay.

What you might be getting at, though, is what's cool with our scrapers, the sophistication of them, is that if a property's hiding availability, whether it be a marketing tactic or, like, a lease up, they don't want to show all 300 units on the page.
Yep.
Our scrapers can actually get around hidden availability.
So, long story short with all of this is if you don't get availability right, all of these performance metrics are wrong.
So that's something that we kind of hang our hat on in terms of accuracy and being unique in the industry.
Okay.
Yeah.
Yeah, and what's nice about this, too, is you can drill into all these metrics.
So if you want to see the 21 units available at 1200 Broadway, you can click into here, see those 21 units.
If you want to see the 4 units that they've leased over the last 7 days, you can see those 4 units that we're saying they have leased.
Hmm.
So, yeah, easy to kind of drill in and be transparent with the data.
We also show pricing and availability here, which is gonna be broken out by floor plan.
So, again, you get both your gross and net rents, you get availability.
This is just very high level, like a quick and dirty, you know, where are my one bedrooms, where are my two bedrooms positioned compared to the comps.
We'll also show fees and deposits, so help you be aware of the ancillary fees and revenue opportunities out there.
Right?
Lastly, on this market survey will be in-unit and community-level amenities.
That's great, yeah.
Yeah.
So, that's the market survey, and then just so you're aware, if you do want to edit comps.

All you have to do is go to the top right and click on Edit Comps, and from here, what we do is, one, give you a map view of the area, so the blue pins are your property, the white pins are properties already added to your comp set, the black pins are properties in the area you might want to consider adding as well.
It's as simple as clicking Add Comp, all of the data plus the historicals are added to that comp set.
Over here on the left, this is going to be a list view, and the major call-out here is that we do assign a score, a comp score, to every property in the vicinity of yours.
And this is just a… it's a correlation score, or a reference point, basically, in terms of how strong our system thinks a property is in comparison to yours.
So, they're objectively… I'd say they're directionally correct.
They're based off of things like vintage, distance, total units, pricing, and amenities.
So I think it's a good reference point, but at the end of the day, you can add or remove whoever you want, you can track as few or as many properties as you'd like as well.
Yeah.
So, that's the market survey.
Our floor plan report here, we present floor plan level data two ways.
The first way is we can just show you the averages by each bed type.
So, you can see at 805 Leah, their average studio is 508 feet, studios make up a quarter of that property, and then this is their leased availability and rent trend data over the last 7 days.
You can pick and choose whatever timeframe you care about, though.
If you toggle over, what's nice is we'll actually break out each individual floor plan within a property.
So a lot of our customers like to have this open, you know, during pricing discussions, but the idea, for example, is that not all one-bedrooms compete with all other one-bedrooms.
So for this example, we can see the smallest one-bedroom in the comp set starts at 516 feet at the alcove.
If I scroll all the way to the bottom, the largest one's gonna be closer to 1,000 square feet here at the Harlow.
So we just kind of want to make it very easy to compare your one-bedrooms off of, like, or similar size 1 bedrooms.
And then same idea, you know, we could show you unit mix at the floor plan level, as well as lease availability and rent trend data.
I think what's interesting here, too, if you toggle on a specific property, like this Camden Musa Grow property.
These guys have, like, 18 or 17 unique one-bedrooms in the property.
But if you look, like, the A4 makes up 11% of the property, the A9 makes up about 12%, so even though there's 17 different one-bedrooms, 2 of them alone make up about a quarter of the property.
So you can almost, in a sense, filter out the noise there.
And what else is cool, too, is you click into a floor plan name, and we can give you a little, a modal here, and we'll show you rent trends at the floor plan level.
So green is gross, blue is, is net.
Anywhere there's a gap in the data just means they didn't have any A9s available during that time frame.
… If you go to historical, so again, we expose our entire 5 years' worth of historical data in here, so you can pick and choose your timeframe.
You can select your interval.
And then we do show debt at the property level, or broken up by floor plan.
And again, inside of here's your rent trends, so both gross and net.

Right now, we're comparing each property's individual performance over that given time frame, but if you'd like, you can toggle over and look at your property's performance compared to the meeting of the comp set.
So you get your rent trends.
You also get that supply history that I was talking… I'm sorry, the, concession history that I was mentioning earlier.
You get supply, Leasing velocity trends.
And also your occupancy, lease, and exposure percentage trends as well.
So… this has been pretty helpful.

It seems like post-pandemic, we're back to some normalization in terms of, you know, seasonality and trends, so it's been really helpful, especially in those markets where there is new supply hitting, You know, unfortunately, the new supply is not necessarily coming on during peak leasing season, so just being able to understand, you know, the different, … Different, different properties performances.
Yeah, for sure.
So… so that's kind of the market survey portion of the tool.
Part 2 is called research, and this is for acquisition and new development.

And I know you said acquisitions aren't necessarily at the forefront right now, but down the road, if you guys would like to, maybe underwrite a property, what this tool does is let you generate one of those market surveys I just walked you through for any property in the country in about 3 clicks.

So all you would do is click on New Report, you would type in the name or the address of whatever property you're looking at, potentially acquiring, you find in the list on here, you click on it, and then again, in 3 seconds, you have all that data I just walked you through.
Cool.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Final part here is Explore, and this is going to be, like, kind of CoStar-esque, but it's all markets.
Market data, so you can type in any city, town, neighborhood, zip code, county, you can draw your own boundary.
So, I'm based out of Phoenix, so I selected Phoenix, but in this example, for the Phoenix city limits, we're tracking about 200,000 units.
Just know we have every filtering option possible, so you can really hone in on exactly what you do care about, whether it be Vintage, total units, property class, etc.
But inside of here, we're going to give you, one, rent comp data.
That's going to include historical rent growth.
Which can be looked at quarterly, monthly, or monthly, quarterly, or yearly.
You can look at both gross and net rents.
It's gonna also include a 30-day snapshot, which includes performance metrics and averages broken out by unit type.
And then you do get those same historicals that we just talked… touched on a second ago as well.
So rent trends, supply by bed type, leasing velocity by bed type, and your occupancy, lease, and exposure percentage trends.
Looking at the map there, you'll notice 3 different colored pins.
So black pins represent properties that exist.
The yellow and pink pins represent properties under different stages of construction.
So you can be aware of the new supply that's about to hit in the areas.
If you put your mouse over those pins, we'll give you details on the construction project.
And on the left, if you scroll down, we'll actually give you the amount of units and properties that make up each stage of construction, as well as when they're slated to be delivered by quarter.
So, yeah.
Very cool.
Yeah, finally here, we do include demographics, so population, education, income, employment data.

And what I've heard that's unique about our demographics is rather than just giving them to you at the market or sub-market level, you can actually take ours down to, like, the neighborhood or zip code, or even custom-drawn boundary, and get the data at a more specific area, so… I always say, like, like Scottsdale, for example, if you get the data for Scottsdale, it's a predominantly older demographic, pre-retirees, but if you look at the data in Old Town, Scottsdale becomes young, professional, single-family households.
So, obviously, you can tell a much better story based off of where… the area you actually care about.
Final part of this that I'll show you, which I think is pretty cool, if I toggle over to properties here, what this does is it shows you every property in your defined area.
But if I click into V-Tree, for example, here, what I can do is I can show you units, property, unit, and amenity-specific images.
I can also give you ownership information, ownership history, transactional data, building specs.
Down below here, I make it easy to compare performance based off of your subject property, based off of V-tree, its direct comps, and the market as a whole.
I like that we lay out unit mixture really well here, so you can see 1-2s, and threes, or if you want, you can expand it and see each unique 1, 2, or 3 in the property.
We just released this.
This is, like, an estimated rent roll.
And what it does, and the way this works, is we know how many units are in each property by floor plan.
And we can show you how many our scrapers have seen.
So, based off of this data, we can show you in-place rents, we can give you that rent rule based off of… You know, all the data we've seen.
Are we able to export any of this out?
Not this part of it.
Not Explore.
You can export the, the market survey data, but not… not what you're seeing right here, unfortunately.
Okay.
Yeah.
I'm just… I'm just actually speaking in generalities, so can you show, can you show us what is able to be exported?
Yep.
So, first off, as far as the data goes here, you can… each data table in here, can be exportable, so if you want to export, like.
anything.
You can export, like, individual data tables, you can pull individual unit-level data out of here, but we do have, like, a pre-canned Excel report.
Okay.
What it looks like is that you get a cover page here, you know, for the property, you get a timestamp for the date that it was downloaded.
Then down below here, you can see there's 6 different data, or 6 different tabs.
One of them being that market survey, which I walked you through.
So, nice, clean, professional look to it.
Yep.
You also get a visual market survey, which the data can be looked at at the property level, or broken up by floor plan.
But this is gonna be about a dozen kind of visuals, letting you compare every major KPI that you could be interested in.
The second one is you get both floor plan reports.
So one, you get the roll-ups, and you can see the averages, for each individual floor plan.
Sorry, you can see the average studio in each property, the average one-bedroom, etc.
The other one here breaks out each individual floor plan, so you can see all the studios are all the one-bedrooms in the property.
plus their, their performance over whatever time frame you care about.
So mine's showing the last 7 days, but if you want to see it over the last month, the last quarter, etc, you can pick and choose that.
And then what else I like is inside of here, we do provide you with all of the raw unit-level data behind what's powering these reports.
So.
Anybody who wants to do any further analysis, create pivot tables, it's just all here.
So….
That's fair.
These are our pre-canned reports.
Okay.
Yeah.
But, yeah, I mean, that's… that's essentially apartment IQ there.
So, like I keep saying, I mean, it's a tool that we're trying to build for every level of the organization.

I think adding in things like… like I was showing you here with ownership information, transactional data, cap rates, rent rolls, I think that this is something that, again, can be that one-stop shop for everything multifamily.
Yeah.
No, that… a lot of… a lot of useful information there, for sure.
Yeah.
And I definitely see, you know, value in that for our organization.

You know, I think… Hunter, we've, talked about this, and Justin, for your knowledge, like, just historically, we've done this stuff in-house, … a lot of manual, reports, and we recognize the need for this.

It's, you know, kind of convincing ownership, … to, … make the expenditure and showing the value, so… and while we've probably made a little headway, we're still not 100% of the way there, but we're having the conversation, hence this demo.

… And, you know, so I think we're moving in that direction, and I think as things progress here, we're definitely… we're going to go from, we like it to, like, we really need this information, because like you said, I mean, as we grow from a… if it's land acquisition or, you know, existing property acquisition, a lot of these reports will help us, as well as helping us set our rates on a, you know, ongoing basis, internally, so… What… can you guys remind me what your… How your pricing structure works, just a little refresh on that.
Yeah, for sure.
Typically we do annual contracts, and then pricing structure, you get unlimited users, and it's on a per unit, per month basis.
So, costs associated with the number of your own units that you're bringing online.
I don't remember off the top of my head the portfolio size.
Do you know, kind of, like, a rough estimate of that?
We… with our new construction that we've got coming online right now, we're gonna be at about $14,200, once that's complete, so we're gonna… we're in lease-up right now on that one.
Okay.
Yeah, 14,000, you'd be looking at, 45 cents per unit per month.
Good.
And again, that's, access to everything that we walked you through today, and then unlimited user access for your entire organization.
And then there's always, like, rules and regulations for who can view what, on the setup.
Okay, okay.
… Okay.
Also, one thing to note, the implementation is extremely easy, not a huge lift at all.
All we need is the list of your assets, the list of all the comps.
We do all the work of building everything out, and it usually takes us about 10 to 14 business days, and then after that, you're up and running.
We can schedule trainings, whether it's per region or per seniority in the company.
We offer unlimited trainings, ongoing trainings.
And, different workshops.
So, I've been at a couple different, kind of, software companies, and this is the easiest implementation process I've ever seen.
Yeah, I mean, it seems like, beyond training, yeah, it seems like it's pretty straightforward, you know, just getting everyone able to use it.
And I can't… I mean, there's no real need for integration with property management software, right?
No, it's a standalone.
Yeah.
Okay, okay.
… Do you have any questions on this, Scott?

I mean, regarding the accuracy of the information you're getting, we… Like, how… what… what is your error rate for, you know, that you're… that you guys are seeing with the information that's coming in?
How do you identify that?
less than 1%, so I'll tell ya, I mean, we're mirroring what's out there.

What's interesting is… the errors would come from, like, the input side by the operators, so… I'll give you an example of, like, an error that happens, is, like, there was a property up in Rhode Island this summer, whatever, a few months back, and they had a one-bedroom for rent for $1.
4 million.
So what happened was that goes into our system, we have, like.
you know, flags, basically.
Our system's like, that's not right.
So we went and checked it out, and here, like, they keyed it in wrong.
You know, it was supposed to be $1,400 a month.
So in that instance, I mean, we actually reached out to the community and said, hey, you know, this is… you guys made a mistake, and they updated it.
But, I mean, yeah, what's nice with this is, I mean, we're just, like, mirroring what's out there.
We're not, like.
you know, doing anything kind of crazy.

I mean, our calculations are based off of availability and based off of, you know, total units and everything, so it's not necessarily errors on our end, it'd be errors on, like, the input side, but it's less than 1% overall.
Makes perfect sense, Julio.
Yeah, bringing that up, it kind of… a thought popped into my head.
When you guys are showing, like, let's say a lease.
asking… so… so the data you're providing, I assume, is, since it's public, it's asking.
You're not able to determine what the actual closed effective is from that perspective, right?

And then with that in mind, for… and we don't do this To a large degree, yet it's only… to a small scale, but, like, revenue management, let's say, you know, if you've got, various different asking rents for lease terms, how does that work into your guys' model?
Yeah, so what we're gonna do for… Like, the pricing matrix, yeah, if they offer different terms, is we're always going to report, based off the lowest advertised price, regardless of term.
Okay, okay.
And same idea with a lease unit.
So we do report a lease price, but it's going to be based off of the last lowest advertised price on the property website, so….
Okay.

Yeah, that's… the rea… what's interesting is we actually have… we can… we can scrape those pricing matrices, and we actually store that data on the back end, but we don't know if they transact that at a 3-month lease, or a 6-month lease, or an 18-month lease.
Exactly.
Kind of the best to normalize the data.
shut up.
That makes sense, that makes sense.
Yeah, I mean, that's typically what we're doing, just manually now.
And it popped into my head, because I know sometimes it can… when you're talking about that $1.
4 million, you don't usually see that big of a disparity, but you can see, you know, a double, you know, a certainly Term might be double what the most, the cheapest one is, so, yeah.

Yeah, I mean, Hunter and I are in Scottsdale, and it's interesting looking at, like, all these expiration curves and understanding, you know, all the supply coming on board, and some of those… some of those terms are significant changes to kind of get back on track, so….
Yeah, yeah.
4 million drastic, but they can be a thousand bucks or even more sometimes, so….
Yeah, God.
Interesting.
Okay.
